nativeRange.habitat: Tropical coral reefs across the Indo-Pacific. Unlike its close relatives S. gigantea and S. haddoni — both of which bury their foot in sand — S. mertensii attaches its pedal disc to hard substrate, typically wedging its foot into rock crevices or holes in coral rubble. Its broad oral disc drapes over the surrounding rock surface, conforming to the contours of coral heads or large boulders.

decorPreferences: large stable rockwork with crevices for pedal-disc attachment
open horizontal rock surface for the oral disc to drape across
high water quality with low nitrate (<10 ppm)
no corals or other anemones within 30 cm of the expected disc footprint
strong but indirect lighting at the attachment point

diet.notes: Merten's Carpet Anemone is primarily photosynthetic through symbiotic zooxanthellae — the single-celled algae that live in its tissues and provide energy through light — but supplemental feeding is generally advised two to four times per week. It has been reported to prefer chunks of fresh or frozen fish flesh such as silversides, marine whitefish, or lancefish over crustacean meat like shrimp or krill, a behavioural tendency noted anecdotally as a distinguishing trait from S. gigantea and S. haddoni. Pieces should be small enough to be drawn fully onto the oral disc and not regurgitated within an hour or two.

goodWith: Amphiprion fuscocaudatus (specialist host — exclusive in the wild)
Amphiprion latifasciatus (specialist host — exclusive in the wild)
avoidWith: most fish not listed as natural clownfish hosts — risk of being captured and consumed
stony and soft corals placed near the anemone's footprint
slow-moving or bottom-dwelling fish (gobies, blennies, dragonets) that may make contact with the disc
shrimp and ornamental crustaceans that may wander into the tentacles

imageCandidates.notes: Scott Mills, 3968x2976. Sharp in-situ shot of the whole anemone draped over rock, showing the green beaded oral disc and the diagnostic red/purple verrucae along the column margin; best diagnostic match.
Ryan McMinds, 1024x683. Striking wide reef scene of the carpet anemone draped over a rock slope with hosting clownfish in blue water; shows the rock-attaching habit hobbyists recognise.
Frederic Ducarme, 4000x3000. Vibrant green oral disc with a hosting Amphiprion latifasciatus pair and egg patch; whole anemone in frame, adds social/host context.

summary: Merten's Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla mertensii) is the largest sea anemone commonly kept in reef aquariums, with an oral disc that can reach 1 m across. Unlike related carpet anemones, it anchors to rock rather than sand, and hosts at least 13 clownfish species. It requires expert-level care and is genuinely rare in the ornamental trade.
careNotes:
Merten's Carpet Anemone (Stichodactyla mertensii) is the largest of the three commonly recognised carpet anemones — wild specimens have been documented with oral discs over 1 m (39 in) in diameter, making it among the largest sea anemones in the world. It is also the rarest of the three in the ornamental trade and is generally considered the most difficult of the carpets to keep long-term.

• Substrate / attachment: Unlike Stichodactyla gigantea and S. haddoni — both of which bury their pedal disc in sand — S. mertensii attaches to hard substrate. In the wild it wedges its foot into rock crevices or holes in coral rubble, with the oral disc draped over the surrounding rock surface. Aquascaping should provide a large flat or gently curved rock area with a deeper crevice for the foot. A deep sand bed is not needed, and attempting to place this species on a sand bed is generally regarded as a common cause of poor settling and wandering. An oral disc that can reach 1 m across requires a tank with correspondingly generous floor dimensions to give the anemone room to expand and settle without crowding corals or other invertebrates.

• Lighting: Strong reef-grade lighting is essential to sustain the zooxanthellae. Specific PAR values are not consistently documented for this species; the husbandry consensus is 'high' equivalent to roughly 200–400 PAR at the anemone's location. Older T5 / metal halide setups (5000–10000 K) have been used successfully; modern reef LEDs of equivalent intensity work as well.

• Flow: Moderate to strong, non-laminar flow is generally advised. Direct laminar blasting onto the disc has been reported to keep the anemone retracted; alternating flow patterns work best.

• Tentacles and sting: The tentacles of S. mertensii are notably short (1–2 cm) and the species has the trade nickname 'Non-Stinging Carpet Anemone' because its sting feels weak to a human fingertip relative to S. haddoni or S. gigantea. This nickname is misleading: the anemone is still a potent predator capable of capturing and consuming reef fish that contact the oral disc, and its sting can cause coral tissue damage on contact. Treat it as dangerous to non-symbiont tankmates regardless of its reputation.

• Clownfish hosting: This species has the broadest documented clownfish host range of any anemone — at least 13 anemonefish species use it in the wild, including Amphiprion clarkii (the most common host in captivity) and the two specialists Amphiprion fuscocaudatus and A. latifasciatus, which associate almost exclusively with S. mertensii. A. ocellaris and A. percula are sometimes reported to host it in captivity but the pairing is not natural.

• Compatibility / tankmate safety: The disc is large enough to capture and consume small to medium reef fish that make contact. Slow-moving or bottom-dwelling fish (gobies, blennies, dragonets, sleeper gobies) and ornamental shrimp are at serious risk. Only symbiotic clownfish from the list above should be considered safe.

• Spacing from other invertebrates: Generally advised to maintain at least 30 cm of clearance from any coral or other anemone. The disc footprint can be much larger than a freshly imported small specimen suggests.

• Color variants: The column is typically tan to white with longitudinal rows of orange/purple verrucae; the oral disc colour varies (brown, green, grey, occasionally striking purple or blue forms). A 'strawberry base' form (deep red column) is occasionally traded at premium prices.

• Acclimation and shipping: Like other large anemones, S. mertensii is highly sensitive to shipping stress and benefits from slow drip acclimation over 1–2 hours. Newly arrived specimens may take days to fully open and weeks to settle into a permanent location. Rapidly wandering specimens are at risk of being shredded by powerheads — intake guards on all pumps are essential.

• Trade availability: Genuinely rare. S. haddoni tends to be the carpet anemone most commonly offered for sale, and all three Stichodactyla species are prone to misidentification in a retail setting — particularly when sold simply as 'carpet anemone' without species-level labelling. True S. mertensii is the least commonly seen of the three and sometimes arrives in shipments ordered as unspecified carpet anemones. Verify identification by tentacle length (very short, 1–2 cm) and substrate preference (rock-attaching, not sand-burying) before purchase.

Direct page fetch was blocked (HTTP 403) but the species summary obtained via search-engine indexing matched aquariumbreeder.com on all high-stakes fields.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Stichodactyla mertensii
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Stichodactylidae, Order Actiniaria), Indo-Pacific native range (Mauritius to Fiji, Ryukyus to Australia), confirmed >1 m oral disc diameter, 1–2 cm tentacle length, list of 13 anemonefish host species including the two S. mertensii specialists A. fuscocaudatus and A. latifasciatus, and the obligate-zooxanthellae detail.
